text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Amber Rutter grabbed shooting silver for Team GB months after giving birth. She tells of her fury at being denied gold - and how she overcame Tokyo heartbreak to get back to the top

There is, in the gentle prompts of a new life, an affirmative gurgle, the hint of a smile, nature’s answer to any disappointment. The presence of four-month-old Tommy lying contentedly behind her set Amber Rutter’s morning in serene pastel shades, allowing a little time to process what has been a giddy period.

Olympic silver and a first child in the space of a few overwhelming months, plus the Greek wedding of her brother three weeks after the birth, pushed Rutter to what for others would be unimaginable limits. Yet, as the special ones discover, there are few hurdles high enough to contain them when they resolve to pursue their dreams.

Oh, and the cheat code that is technology. Tommy’s restful state was brought about by the magic of television and a screen of gently swaying bananas. “Dancing fruit. Saw it on TikTok. Apparently they get mesmerised by it. That and a dummy,” she said.

If only the shooting authorities and the IOC were as amenable to Rutter’s appeal for good behaviour. Three weeks aftergold was cruelly denied herwhen the judgesfailed to spotwhat the rest of the world could see – a piece of splintered clay confirming a hit during the shoot-out for skeet gold – still no word or apology.

“That has really disappointed me. I never wanted the medals to be changed. I never wanted there to be a different outcome. However I do feel it wasn’t just me. There were other girls affected,” Rutter toldi.

“No one can understand if you can see on an iPhone, how on Earth has the ref not been able to see it? It reflected badly on our sport. An apology is needed that says, ‘You know what, we got it wrong, we should have had VAR, but we didn’t. Moving forward these are the steps in place to stop something like this happening again.’

“It’s a gamechanger for our sport. I wouldn’t want it to happen to anyone else.”

Happily, that silver medal still felt like a win. How could it not when in the moment of claiming it she turned unexpectedly to see her son in the arms of husband James, who had driven down to Châteauroux the day before to surprise her?

For a woman who had struggled through the disappointment ofTokyo, where a positive Covid test the day before her departure erased a whole Olympic cycle and left her bereft, this was indeed a fairy-tale ending.

“I was ready to quit the sport. I said I didn’t want to continue. I didn’t think I could do it all again. This time around I prioritised my family, Tommy. Training came second to all of that. Going into the Olympics I wasn’t feeling my best, but I just thought, ‘You know what I’m just going to give it my best and whatever will be will be.’

“Being a mum and going to the Olympics is not easy. I have had many days when I have really struggled and wondered If I’m doing the right thing here, but I just keep showing up and being the best mum possible, and doing my best in training. It’s tiring but I hope it shows other mums that life does not have to stop when you have kids. And getting silver has been such an amazing experience.

“An Olympic medal is something you work for. You have to peak for two days. I didn’t think that was going to be possible so to come away with a medal of any colour was just so pleasing. The most important thing was turning around and seeing Tommy. That felt like gold to me. He is just my whole world.”

There is an important message in Rutter’s story regarding mental health, an issue impossible to quantify or package neatly. Her response to that crushing Covid test in 2021 is a lesson in taking seriously how others respond to crisis. However people feel about a situation, their emotions are real, and they are entitled to them.

“If I’m honest I don’t think Tokyo is something I will ever fully get over. It was the worst point of my life. Never experienced pain and upset like it. Straight afterwards I felt like I was dealing with it, but once the whole thing died down and you go into the winter months.

“It’s something called the Olympic blues. You have all this build-up to the games and then everyone forgets about it. That was the point I really struggled. I put myself in such a bad place. I didn’t want to do my sport anymore. I just wanted to be an average person working nine to five.”

Seeking the necessary help was key. “I went to counselling. I am not ashamed to talk about it. I don’t like to label it as depression or anxiety. I am generally a very happy person, and look at the positives, but I think we go through times where we struggle, when there are certain situations or relationship problems or whatever it may be. But I have never experienced anything like I went through with Tokyo. I was really sad most days and lost with what direction I wanted to take in my life.”

Shooting is not football or tennis. She didn’t have the luxury of quitting. “The only reason I continued was for money, to keep a roof over my head and pay my mortgage. It took me a good one to two years to accept what had happened and learn to look to the future instead of living in the past. I had a responsibility to my sponsors and lottery funding. I was lucky and grateful to have that, and it helped get me get through.

“I know it sounds cheesy and we thank our sponsors a lot, but I would not be shooting without them. That’s the reality for us. We are definitely looked after. I have some great sponsors like Clays, Beretta and Eley Hawk. Without them I couldn’t continue in the sport. We are not like the big sports.”

Not yet. Rutter hopes her story, the rise from the trauma of Tokyo, the controversy in Paris, will bring new eyes to shooting. And she has a new clothing line to spice up her life, and maybe yours. “It’s called Ukiyo. I was looking for something that represented my journey, from the struggles of Tokyo to this next chapter in my life. It’s Japanese for living in the moment, detaching from life’s problems. I instantly knew it was the right name.

“I have designed a range – shooting vests, waterproofs, leggings etc. The idea is to change the stereotypes around our sport, all the tweed, the male farmer. I want to encourage young people to get into this sport. You don’t have to wear tweed and wellies to shoot.

“Personally I don’t like tweed, I don’t like green, I don’t like wellies. I like my trainers and active wear. When I put on my shooting jacket I want to feel positive. If you look good and feel good it gives you the confidence to go out and perform.”

Amber Rutter is a brand ambassador for Clays, London’s most advanced virtual shooting experience.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: I was robbed of Olympic gold in Paris - and no one has apologised
author: Kevin Garside
description: Amber Rutter grabbed shooting silver for Team GB months after giving birth. She tells of her fury at being denied gold - and how she overcame Tokyo heartbreak to get back to the top
